speaker: Host
text_en: Will AI replace software engineers?
text: AI 会取代软件工程师吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I am highly skeptical. Who's going to make the AI? AI is not going to innovate. Someone has to design the CPU and the operating system. AI is not going to do that.
text: 我对此非常怀疑。那谁来创造 AI 呢？AI 本身不会创新。总得有人去设计 CPU 和操作系统。AI 可做不了这个。

speaker: Host
text_en: What are your opinions on vibe coding?
text: 你怎么看 vibe coding？

speaker: Guest
text_en: That doesn't really bring a lot to the table anyway. That code has to come from somewhere. It's not all going to be generated by AI.
text: 那东西其实也带不来太多价值。代码总得有来源，不可能全都是 AI 生成的。

speaker: Host
text_en: When people are learning to code, what are some common mistakes you see people make?
text: 大家在学编程的时候，你常看到哪些常见错误？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Not spending enough time. How this industry makes progress.
text: 投入的时间不够。这不是这个行业进步的方式。

speaker: Host
text_en: What was the story of you creating C-sharp?
text: 你创造 C-sharp 的故事是怎样的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, the story probably starts like with my
text: 嗯，这个故事大概得从我的……开始讲起。

speaker: Host
text_en: Today I'm sitting down with Anders Halsberg, a technical fellow at Microsoft, to talk about the future of coding and AI. If you're in computer science or are a tech beginner, you need to pay attention. Because Anders is not just a tech leader, but he's the literal creator of programming languages C-sharp and TypeScript. And so when he talks about AI and coding,
text: 今天我请到了 Microsoft 的 technical fellow Anders Halsberg，一起来聊聊编程和 AI 的未来。如果你在学计算机科学，或者你是科技领域的新手，那你一定要认真听。因为 Anders 不只是技术领袖，他还是编程语言 C-sharp 和 TypeScript 的真正创造者。所以当他谈 AI 和编程时，

speaker: Host
text_en: he is speaking from decades worth of experience. And he dispels a lot of the lies and nonsense people have been spewing about AI. Plus, if you want to succeed and actually get ahead of most development,
text: 他说的话是建立在几十年经验之上的。而且他也戳破了很多人一直在散布的关于 AI 的谎言和胡扯。另外，如果你想成功，真的在大多数开发者中脱颖而出，

speaker: Host
text_en: Developers, can you share some crazy behind the scenes stories from building C-sharp and TypeScript and I am confident that will change the way you think about coding projects going into the future. So a lot of value to unpack in this video and let's get to it right away. In the world of AI that we live in today, a lot of people are even questioning is it worth learning how to code?
text: 你能不能分享一些当年打造 C-sharp 和 TypeScript 时特别疯狂的幕后故事？我很确定，这会改变大家未来看待编程项目的方式。所以这期视频里有很多很有价值的内容可以挖，我们马上开始。活在今天这个 AI 世界里，很多人甚至都在质疑：现在还有必要学编程吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Oh, I absolutely think it's worth learning how to code. Programming languages and code is how AI expresses itself. And there's a whole different world from AI. right below that has to exist that ai assumes the existence of in the code that it generates that code has to come from somewhere it's not all going to be generated by ai i
text: 哦，我绝对认为学编程是值得的。编程语言和代码，就是 AI 表达自己的方式。而且在 AI 之下，其实还有一个完全不同的世界必须存在，AI 生成代码时默认这个世界已经在那里了。那些代码总得有来源，不可能所有东西都由 AI 生成。

speaker: Host
text_en: What are your opinions on vibe coding is going to replace coders?
text: 你怎么看现在那种所谓的 vibe coding 会取代程序员这种说法？

speaker: Guest
text_en: am highly skeptical for a lot of stuff that is wrote how many times can you write this to-do list app ai in its training set has seen a gazillion times so it can riff over that and it can actually get more more creative now but when it comes down to business logic Logic or you got to invent something. That's how this industry makes progress right if that's not gonna come true, from AI.
text: 我对此非常怀疑。很多东西都已经被写过无数遍了。像这种 to-do list app，AI 在训练集里看过几十亿次，所以它当然可以在这上面发挥，甚至现在还能更有创意一些。但一旦涉及业务逻辑，或者你必须发明一些新东西，这才是这个行业取得进步的方式，对吧？而这部分不会由 AI 来实现。

speaker: Host
text_en: Like understanding the fundamentals. Yesterday I got the opportunity to have a quick chat with Satya. I had asked him beginner level skills people need to know when they're getting into tech. He mentioned understanding the architecture.
text: 比如理解那些基础原理。昨天我有机会和 Satya 简单聊了几句。我问他，刚进入科技行业的人需要掌握哪些入门级技能。他提到要理解架构。

speaker: Host
text_en: That's something AI can help you generate the code, but it won't do the fundamental designs which comes from the human mind. And you designed languages such as C-sharp and TypeScripts and were very cognizant. Synthesize what problems you were trying to solve. Rewinding the clock a little bit, what was the story of you creating C-Sharp?
text: 这些东西 AI 可以帮你生成代码，但它做不了那些根本性的设计，而那是来自人类思维的。你设计过 C-sharp 和 TypeScript 这样的语言，而且你当时一定非常清楚自己想解决什么问题。把时间稍微往回拨一点，你当初创造 C-Sharp 的故事是什么？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, the story probably starts like with my previous endeavor there at a company called Borland where I wrote Turbo Pascal and then worked also as the architect of a product called Delphi. This was all about making programmers more productive, rapid application development, and we competed with Microsoft's marquee product Visual Basic.
text: 嗯，这个故事大概要从我之前在一家叫 Borland 的公司做的项目说起。我当时写了 Turbo Pascal，后来还担任了一个叫 Delphi 的产品的架构师。这些工作本质上都是为了让程序员更高效，也就是快速应用开发；我们当时竞争的对象是 Microsoft 的明星产品 Visual Basic。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You know, if you can't beat them, join them. So I joined Microsoft in 96. There was this thing called Visual Basic that was super easy to write apps in. And then there was this thing called C++ that where all the expressiveness and performance was available. And people really wanted a little bit
text: 你知道，打不过就加入。所以我在 96 年加入了 Microsoft。当时有个叫 Visual Basic 的东西，写应用特别容易。然后还有个叫 C++ 的东西，所有的表达力和性能都在那里。大家其实很想要一点

speaker: Host
text_en: of right.
text: 两者兼得，对吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: both. And so we set out to create that sort of the best of both worlds, you know, something that gave you the power and expressiveness of C++ but with the ease of use of Visual Basic with a bunch of modern infrastructure behind. behind it called .NET Are
text: 没错，两者都要。所以我们就开始着手打造这样一种“两个世界的最佳结合”，也就是既有 C++ 的能力和表达力，又有 Visual Basic 的易用性，背后还配上一整套现代化基础设施，也就是 .NET。

speaker: Host
text_en: Are there any examples of features of the language that did both so you could switch?
text: 有没有什么语言特性的例子，能体现这种两者兼得、可以灵活切换的感觉？

speaker: Guest
text_en: things like properties and events there were object-oriented programming languages already but everyone was working with this notion of properties on their objects and events on their objects but no programming languages had given first-class treatment to them before Do any
text: 像 properties 和 events 这样的东西。那时候其实已经有面向对象编程语言了，但大家都在用对象上的 properties 和对象上的 events 这种概念，可是在那之前，没有哪种编程语言把它们当成一等公民来对待。

speaker: Host
text_en: you have any example of a great project to create with C-sharp?
text: 你能举个适合用 C-sharp 来做的很棒项目例子吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: kind of web back-end or rest API or write a game I mean like unity Right? Like a third of the world's C-sharp programmers write games. It almost doesn't matter what you write. Once you start writing code, you start understanding how programming languages work and how to build data structures and how to get performance,
text: 比如做 web 后端、REST API，或者写游戏。像 Unity，对吧？差不多全球有三分之一的 C-sharp 程序员都在写游戏。其实你写什么几乎都无所谓。一旦你开始写代码，你就会开始理解编程语言是怎么工作的，怎么构建数据结构，以及怎么把性能做好。

speaker: Guest
text_en: how to measure performance. When you're learning, it's less important exactly what you write, but just the fact that you're writing. You know, you could go write a framework or set a collection classes if you wanted to. It doesn't matter.
text: 怎么衡量表现。当你在学习的时候，具体写了什么其实没那么重要，重要的是你在写。你知道，如果你愿意，你完全可以去写一个框架，或者做一组类库。其实都无所谓。

speaker: Host
text_en: Yeah. And do you think that C-sharp would be as big as it is? Because it got to today
text: 对。那你觉得 C# 会发展到今天这么大的规模吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: of course not you never know right i mean and yes c sharp i am very proud that it stood the test of time in fact i was talking to someone earlier today that it's 25 years since we released c sharp at the 2000 professional developers conference so 25 years that's a long time for something to still be around and in widespread use
text: 当然不会，你永远没法预料，对吧。是的，C# 能经受住时间的考验，我对此非常自豪。其实我今天早些时候还在跟人聊，距离我们在 2000 年的 Professional Developers Conference 上发布 C#，已经过去 25 年了。所以 25 年啊，一个东西还能一直存在，而且还被广泛使用，这已经是很长的时间了。

speaker: Host
text_en: fast forwarding a little into your journey so typescript right can you give us the story of that and what problem you were trying to solve
text: 稍微快进一点，聊聊你职业历程里的下一段。那 TypeScript 呢？你能给我们讲讲它的故事，以及你当时想解决什么问题吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: result
text: 结果。

speaker: Host
text_en: Really quick, as a software engineer, you would think writing code is what slows me down, but it's actually the small tasks that pile up the most. That's when I started using Superhuman Mail,
text: 很快说一句，作为软件工程师，你可能会觉得拖慢我的是写代码，但其实真正最耗时间的是那些越积越多的小任务。也就是从那时起，我开始用 Superhuman Mail。

speaker: Host
text_en: which manages my inbox and keeps me organized without all the back and forth. And now I can connect it to tools like ChatGPT or Claude and actually manage my inbox from there. Let your AI agents run your inbox and calendar. Let me show you how I use this. I connected Superhuman Mail to Claude and now I can run everything from here. Here, one thing I use a lot is cross-tool workflows.
text: 它能帮我管理收件箱，让我保持条理，不用来回折腾。现在我还可以把它连接到 ChatGPT 或 Claude 这样的工具上，直接从那边管理我的收件箱。让你的 AI agents 帮你处理收件箱和日历。我给你演示一下我是怎么用的。我把 Superhuman Mail 连到了 Claude，现在我可以直接在这里处理所有事情。这里有个我经常用的功能，就是跨工具工作流。

speaker: Host
text_en: For example, I can ask it to look through recent emails and summarize anything important, then send that straight into Slack. Or, after meetings, I can have it draft up follow-ups based on my notes without opening my inbox. It removes a lot of the small tasks that usually add up.
text: 比如，我可以让它查看最近的邮件，把重要内容总结出来，然后直接发到 Slack。或者开完会之后，我可以让它根据我的笔记起草后续跟进邮件，连收件箱都不用打开。它省掉了很多原本会一点点累积起来的小任务。

speaker: Host
text_en: If you're afraid of your email sounding too AI-generated, you can insert rules just using no em dashes or ellipses to ensure you're all good to go and that it is in your tone of voice. The beauty is, these rules will apply to your entire network, and you'll be able to use them to your advantage.
text: 如果你担心自己写的邮件听起来太像 AI 生成的，你可以加一些规则，比如不要用破折号或省略号，这样就能确保内容没问题，而且符合你的说话风格。妙就妙在，这些规则会应用到你的整个网络里，你也能把它们用起来为自己加分。

speaker: Host
text_en: Apply every time without having to edit each time to match your voice and tone. The biggest game changer for me was the AI to help me actually prepare for the day. It can collect all the tasks and insights that I have and help me create a to-do list.
text: 每次都会自动套用，不用你每次都手动改成符合自己声音和语气的版本。对我来说，最大的改变是 AI 能真正帮我准备一天的工作。它可以把我的所有任务和洞察整理起来，帮我生成一份待办清单。

Make sure to sign up for the business plan if you want to connect Claw, ChatGPT, and other AI tools. And now back to the video. Well, Timescript, the story probably starts about 50...

speaker: Guest
text_en: 15 years ago when a team at Microsoft the outlook team came to us and I was doing C sharp at the time and they came to us and they asked hey could you guys productize this thing called script shop and I'm like what script shop well it's this thing that takes C sharp and cross compiles it to JavaScript and I'm like okay and why would someone want to do that well we do that because that's the only way that we can get great tooling and scalability for a large
text: 15 年前说起，当时 Microsoft 的 Outlook 团队来找我们，那时候我在做 C sharp。他们来问我们，嘿，你们能不能把一个叫 script shop 的东西产品化？我当时想，什么是 script shop？他们说，就是一个能把 C sharp 交叉编译成 JavaScript 的东西。我说，好吧，那为什么会有人想这么做？他们说，因为这是我们唯一能在大型项目上获得优秀工具支持和可扩展性的办法，而我们正在用 javascript 写一个超大型应用，因为那时候大家都在往浏览器迁移，而 javascript 是真正的跨平台方案。你知道，当时出现了各种各样的设备形态，比如手机和平板。

speaker: Guest
text_en: large project and we're writing this enormous app in javascript because at the time everyone was moving to the browser and javascript as the true cross-platform solution you know there was like a whole diversity of device form factors coming out like phones and tablets
text: 而且已经不能默认它一定会跑在 PC 上了，所以浏览器就是答案。对。再加上 html5 出现了，你确实已经能写出像样的 UI 了，所以……

speaker: Guest
text_en: and the pc wasn't you couldn't just assume that it was going to run on a pc and so browser was it Yeah. and html5 had happened and you could actually write decent ui right and so
text: 所以当时大家都在用 JavaScript 写超大型应用，但写起来非常痛苦。这个团队选择的解决办法是用 C sharp 来写应用，再把它交叉编译成 JavaScript。我当时就想，这也太疯狂了吧。你总不可能靠写 C sharp 来写出最顶级的 JavaScript

speaker: Guest
text_en: So people were just writing enormous apps in JavaScript and having a very hard time doing it. And this team had chosen to solve the problem by writing the app in C sharp and cross compiling it to JavaScript. And I'm like, but that's crazy. Surely you're not going to write best of breed JavaScript
text: 应用吧。你难道不更想直接用 JavaScript 来写吗？那 JavaScript 到底是哪里坏掉了？我们能不能把它修好？所以这大概就是 TypeScript 的起源。某种意义上，就是修好 JavaScript，对吧？让它变得可用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: apps by writing them in C sharp. Wouldn't you rather write them in JavaScript? What is it about JavaScript that's broken and can we fix it? And so that was sort of the genesis of TypeScript. Fix JavaScript, if you will, right? Make it usable.
text: 应用吧。你难道不更想直接用 JavaScript 来写吗？那 JavaScript 到底是哪里坏掉了？我们能不能把它修好？所以这大概就是 TypeScript 的起源。某种意义上，就是修好 JavaScript，对吧？让它变得可用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: for large-scale projects and add those things to the language that are necessary such that we can build great tooling on top and in particular that meant adding a static type system to the language we then enable the ability to write great tooling that you see in say Visual Studio Code
text: 用于大规模项目，并把那些必要的东西加进这门语言里，这样我们就能在它之上构建出很棒的工具，尤其是，这意味着要给这门语言加入静态类型系统。这样一来，我们就具备了打造优秀工具的能力，比如你在 Visual Studio Code 里看到的那些。

speaker: Host
text_en: more
text: 更

speaker: Guest
text_en: and our language
text: 还有我们的语言

speaker: Host
text_en: like
text: 像

speaker: Guest
text_en: service
text: 服务

speaker: Host
text_en: a developer experience in
text: 开发者体验方面的

speaker: Guest
text_en: exactly because that's ultimately where the productivity comes from right it's like having things like statement completion and code navigation and seeing red red squiggly when you make a mistake and whatever that's what developers love that stuff once you give it to them you can never take it away again right so
text: 没错，因为归根结底，生产力就是从这里来的，对吧。比如语句自动补全、代码导航、还有你出错时看到的红色波浪线之类的，这些才是开发者最爱的东西。你一旦给了他们这些，就再也拿不回去了，对吧，所以

speaker: Host
text_en: Python developers might disagree slightly, but yeah,
text: Python 开发者可能会稍微有点不同意见，不过是的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: well they might but you know what I don't know that people are writing million line apps or programs in Python to the same extent once something gets big enough you really do need these things yeah
text: 嗯，也许会吧，但你知道，我不确定人们会在 Python 里同样大量地去写那种上百万行的应用或程序。东西一旦大到一定程度，你确实就需要这些东西了。对。

speaker: Host
text_en: that readability type scripts. Yeah, as the creator of C Sharp and TypeScript, when... People are learning to code. What are some common mistakes you see people make?
text: 就是那种可读性，TypeScript 也是。对。作为 C Sharp 和 TypeScript 的创造者，当大家在学习编程时，你看到人们最常犯的一些错误是什么？

speaker: Guest
text_en: not spending enough time learning the basic principles and really sort of understand what is it I'm doing what is a variable really and what is an array and what is a data structure and how do pointers work once you grok that then it doesn't really matter what language you're in you know that's just a veneer on top of the semantics right but really understanding Diving the semantics deep. is
text: 就是没有花足够多的时间去学习那些基础原理，没有真正搞明白自己到底在做什么。变量到底是什么，数组是什么，数据结构是什么，指针又是怎么工作的。你一旦真正吃透这些，其实你用什么语言就没那么重要了，你知道，语言只是语义之上的一层表皮，对吧？但真正深入理解语义这件事，非常关键。

speaker: Guest
text_en: is super important because that allows you to think in a more abstract fashion if you don't get that deeper understanding of it then you're sort of thinking at that veneer syntactic level of what do i have to write but you're not really understanding why you're writing it
text: 这点特别重要，因为它能让你用一种更抽象的方式去思考。如果你没有这种更深层的理解，那你基本上就只是在语法表层上思考：我该写什么；但你并没有真正理解自己为什么要这么写。

speaker: Host
text_en: Interesting.
text: 有意思。

speaker: Guest
text_en: write
text: 写。

speaker: Host
text_en: If I'm a beginner in tech and I wanted to get hired at a big tech company like Microsoft, what is your number one piece of advice?
text: 如果我是一个刚入行的技术新人，想进 Microsoft 这样的大型科技公司，你最重要的一条建议是什么？

speaker: Guest
text_en: a bunch of open source code put it on github that becomes your resume there's nothing like demonstrating the ability to code and a fantastic way to demonstrate that is by building stuff and putting it out in the open so don't sit on it share it yeah
text: 做一堆开源代码，放到 GitHub 上，那就会成为你的简历。没有什么比直接展示你的编程能力更有说服力了，而展示这一点的绝佳方式，就是把东西做出来，然后公开发布。所以别把它压在手里，分享出来。对。

speaker: Host
text_en: And particularly with AI in the world that we live in, are there certain problems that you would choose to tackle?
text: 尤其是在我们如今这个有 AI 的世界里，有没有哪些问题是你会特别想去解决的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: it depends on what kind of programming excites you because there are many different things you could do some people like building apps the UI aspect of it and the experience of the app Yeah, other people like systems like stuff that doesn't have a UI at all or but is like they're into making it perform incredibly well and you know some people like the theory of it all right
text: 这要看你对哪一类编程更有热情，因为你能做的事情有很多。有些人喜欢做 app，喜欢它的 UI 层面，还有 app 的使用体验。对，另外一些人喜欢系统类的东西，比如完全没有 UI 的那种，或者他们就是很着迷于把性能做到极致。你知道，也有些人喜欢其中的理论部分，对吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: and then so there are many different ways you can dive into it I mean for me I've always been fascinated with writing efficient data structures and understanding how they work how does the hash table work why does it work and how do I make it more efficient how can I structure the data in my program the right way because ultimately that's where the performance comes from right. right
text: 所以你可以从很多不同的方向深入进去。对我来说，我一直很着迷于编写高效的数据结构，并理解它们是怎么工作的。哈希表是怎么工作的？它为什么有效？我又该怎么让它更高效？我怎样才能用正确的方式来组织程序里的数据？因为归根结底，性能就是从这里来的，对吧。对。

speaker: Host
text_en: It's like the small design choices that affect so much in the performance,
text: 就像是一些很小的设计选择，却会对性能产生非常大的影响。

speaker: Guest
text_en: yep
text: 对

speaker: Host
text_en: right? And do you think a computer science degree is worth it?
text: 对吧？那你觉得 computer science 学位值得读吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: oh i i definitely think so you know i was sort of somewhat self-taught because at the time i went to college back in denmark i grew up in denmark the engineering school i went to didn't even have computer science yet they started it the following year right but i was like in the late
text: 哦，我绝对觉得值得。你知道，我某种程度上算是自学成才，因为我上大学那会儿是在 Denmark，我是在 Denmark 长大的。我读的那所工程学院当时甚至还没有 computer science 这个专业，他们是第二年才开设的，对吧。但我那时候已经是在 70 年代末了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The late 70s, you could go to the other university for computer science, but I was doing engineering. And so a lot of things I sort of had to learn by myself by doing. Boy,
text: 70 年代末的时候，你可以去另一所大学读 computer science，但我当时学的是工程。所以很多东西我都得靠自己一边做一边学。天哪。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I could have saved a lot of time by getting an education that allows you to understand like the history of programming and like we've talked about here, like what is a programming language? What are the principles of programming? What is data structures? What are databases? What are operating systems like all of this stuff? It certainly helps to have a passion for it. you
text: 如果能接受一种教育，让你理解编程的历史，以及像我们刚才聊到的这些东西，比如什么是编程语言、编程的原理是什么、什么是数据结构、什么是数据库、什么是操作系统，诸如此类，那我本来可以省下很多时间。当然，对这件事有热情肯定也很有帮助。

speaker: Host
text_en: Mm hmm.
text: 嗯哼。

speaker: Guest
text_en: know for me I sometimes joke I never had a real job because I was always doing my hobby coding was always my hobby and I was fortunate If enough you to love make what you a do, yeah you never work. if you aim to do your best work you there's got to be a bit of passion in it you know no
text: 你知道，对我来说，我有时候会开玩笑说我从来没做过一份真正的工作，因为我一直都在做自己的爱好，写代码一直都是我的爱好。我很幸运，能热爱自己所做的事。是啊，如果你做的是自己热爱的事，你就永远不会觉得自己在工作。如果你想做出最好的成果，那里面总得有一点热情，你懂的。

speaker: Host
text_en: Right. For sure. And one final question, will AI replace software engineers?
text: 对，确实。最后一个问题，AI 会取代软件工程师吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think who's gonna make the AI right and who's gonna make the programming languages that the AI expresses itself in and who's gonna write the frame
text: 我觉得，AI 由谁来做呢，对吧？还有，AI 用来表达自己的那些编程语言由谁来创造？那些框架又由谁来写——

speaker: Guest
text_en: frameworks that the AI talk I mean no AI is an accelerant and it's going to change the way that we do things and it's going to remove a lot of grunt work that we don't need to do anymore because AI can help us do it but it'll allow us to focus more on the creative side because AI is not going to innovate it's not going to like Bing have this crazy idea I had in the shower this morning you know AI doesn't work that way yeah
text: 那些 AI 要对接、要使用的 frameworks 由谁来做？我的意思是，不会。AI 是一种加速器，它会改变我们的做事方式，也会去掉很多我们以后不需要再亲手做的脏活累活，因为 AI 可以帮我们完成。但这反而会让我们更专注在创造性的那一面，因为 AI 不会创新。它不会像“我今天早上洗澡时突然冒出一个疯狂点子”那样运作。AI 不是这么工作的。是的。

speaker: Host
text_en: So right now as a software engineer, we code five years from now when AI is is more into more the workplace. pervasive. What do you see software engineers do?
text: 所以现在，作为软件工程师，我们是在写代码。那五年后，当 AI 更深入、更普遍地进入职场之后，你觉得软件工程师会做什么？

speaker: Guest
text_en: AI will be able to do a lot of that grunt work. But like I said, there's still the deeper side of the science, these frameworks that you have to target. Someone has to design the CPU. Someone has to design the operating system. There's plenty left. Someone has to get the bright idea and devise the right data structure for sharding this app so our startup can grow fast or what have you, right?
text: AI 会能做掉很多那种脏活累活。但就像我说的，科学里更深层的那一面依然还在，还有这些你必须面向的 frameworks。总得有人去设计 CPU，总得有人去设计操作系统。还有很多事情可做。总得有人冒出那个绝妙的点子，设计出合适的数据结构来对这个 app 做分片，这样我们的 startup 才能快速增长，诸如此类，对吧？
